aws.d: Hard blast-radius wall. Multi-account via Organizations + OUs is the norm.

azure.d: Billing + policy boundary, grouped under Management Groups, inside one Entra tenant.

gcp.d: The unit everything lives in, nested under Folders and an Organization.

usage: This is the #1 translation error. An Azure Subscription ≠ AWS account in weight — spinning up Subscriptions is cheap and routine; GCP Projects are even lighter (per app/env). AWS accounts are the heaviest boundary of the three.

aws.d: Named per account (us-east-1a) — the letter maps differently across accounts.

azure.d: Zones 1–3 per region; some services instead use Availability Sets (rack-level).

gcp.d: Named globally and consistently (asia-southeast2-a).

usage: Same word, different guarantees: Azure regions historically didn't all have zones; GCP zone names are stable across projects while AWS AZ letters are shuffled per account (use AZ IDs for cross-account work).

aws.d: Grouping is logical, via tags; AWS Resource Groups are just saved tag queries.

azure.d: Mandatory container — every resource lives in exactly one RG; deleting the RG deletes everything.

gcp.d: The project itself is the group; labels add metadata.

usage: Azure RGs are structural, not cosmetic. Coming from AWS, the shock is that a resource cannot exist outside an RG, and RG-delete is a real (and dangerous) lifecycle operation.

aws.d: JSON/YAML stacks; CDK compiles to it.

azure.d: Bicep is the modern DSL that transpiles to ARM JSON.

gcp.d: Effectively deprecated in practice — Google itself points people to Terraform.

usage: If your team is Terraform-first (yours is), this row mostly matters for reading other people's legacy stacks during migrations.

aws.d: Instance types like t3.medium, m7i.large.

azure.d: VM sizes like Standard_D2s_v5.

gcp.d: Machine types like e2-standard-2; custom machine types are a GCP-only trick.

usage: GCP lets you dial arbitrary vCPU/RAM combos (custom machine types) and live-migrates VMs during host maintenance — there's no AWS-style 'scheduled reboot for maintenance' culture.

aws.d: Amazon Machine Image, region-scoped, copied explicitly across regions.

azure.d: Azure Compute Gallery (ex Shared Image Gallery) handles versioning + replication.

gcp.d: Images are global to the project by default.

usage: AWS AMIs are per-region artifacts you must copy; GCP images are project-global, so multi-region rollout pipelines look very different.

aws.d: Launch template + scaling policies.

azure.d: Flexible orchestration mode is the modern default.

gcp.d: Instance template + autoscaler; regional MIGs spread across zones.

usage: Functionally the closest 1:1:1 mapping in this dictionary. The trap: GCP autoscaler and MIG are separate resources in Terraform, not one block like an ASG.

aws.d: 2-minute interruption notice; price varies by pool.

azure.d: Evicted on capacity/price; supports max-price cap.

gcp.d: 30-second preemption notice; old preemptibles had a hard 24h max life.

usage: Interruption semantics differ — AWS gives 120s warning, GCP gives 30s. Design your drain hooks around the worst case of whichever cloud you're on.

aws.d: Regional. Subnets are zonal. Cross-region = peering or Transit Gateway.

azure.d: Regional. Subnets are region-wide (not zonal). Global VNet Peering crosses regions.

gcp.d: GLOBAL. One VPC spans every region; subnets are regional.

usage: The single biggest mental-model break in cross-cloud networking. 'One VPC per region' is an AWS-ism — a GCP VPC is a global routing domain, and Azure subnets don't pin to zones at all.

aws.d: Zonal; public/private is determined by its route table.

azure.d: Regional slice of the VNet; NSGs and route tables attach here.

gcp.d: Regional; supports secondary IP ranges (the thing your EKS-style pod CIDRs map to).

usage: GCP secondary ranges are first-class on the subnet — the closest analog to the secondary-CIDR-for-pods pattern you've fought with on EKS, but declared up front instead of bolted on.

aws.d: Stateful, attached to ENIs, allow-only rules that can reference other SGs.

azure.d: Stateful, attached to subnets or NICs, has allow AND deny with priorities.

gcp.d: Live on the network, targeted via tags or service accounts — nothing 'attaches' to the VM.

usage: AWS SGs can't deny; NSGs can. GCP has no attached-to-instance object at all — rules select targets by tag/service-account, which flips how you write Terraform modules for it.

aws.d: Zonal appliance you place in a public subnet, per-AZ for HA, billed per hour + per GB.

azure.d: Attached to subnets; zone-resilient options vary.

gcp.d: Software-defined, hangs off a Cloud Router — no appliance, no subnet placement.

usage: Cloud NAT has no 'public subnet' concept to babysit and no per-AZ duplication. The AWS pattern of one NAT GW per AZ for HA simply doesn't translate.

aws.d: Non-transitive, pairwise.

azure.d: Also non-transitive; Global Peering crosses regions.

gcp.d: Peering is non-transitive too, but Shared VPC lets many projects share one host network.

usage: All three peerings are non-transitive — but GCP's Shared VPC sidesteps the whole hub-and-spoke dance for intra-org cases: service projects just use the host project's network.

aws.d: Regional hub, peered across regions; the backbone of multi-VPC architectures.

azure.d: Managed global transit with vHubs per region.

gcp.d: Hub-and-spoke over Google's backbone; less needed because VPCs are already global.

usage: Your multi-account TGW architecture translates to vWAN on Azure almost 1:1. On GCP you may not need a hub at all — one global VPC + Shared VPC often replaces the entire pattern.

aws.d: Interface + gateway endpoints; S3/Dynamo gateway endpoints are free.

azure.d: Private IP for a PaaS service inside your VNet; pairs with Private DNS zones.

gcp.n: Private Service Connect / Private Google Access
gcp.d: PSC for producer/consumer; PGA gives subnets Google-API access with no endpoint objects.

usage: Azure Private Endpoints demand Private DNS zone wiring that surprises AWS folks (the DNS is the hard part, not the endpoint). GCP's Private Google Access needs zero per-service endpoints for Google APIs.

aws.d: One resource + listeners + target groups. Regional.

azure.d: Regional L7 with WAF SKU; Front Door is the global L7 layer.

gcp.d: Not one resource — a chain: forwarding rule → target proxy → URL map → backend service. Globally anycast by default.

usage: GCP load balancers aren't appliances, they're 5–6 chained Terraform resources. Painful the first time, but you get a single global anycast IP — the thing AWS needs Global Accelerator to fake.

aws.d: Zones + fancy routing policies (weighted, latency, failover) + health checks.

azure.d: Zones; Traffic Manager handles the routing-policy tricks.

gcp.d: Zones incl. private zones; routing policies exist but are thinner.

usage: Route 53's routing policies are a bundled superpower — on Azure that's a separate product (Traffic Manager), so a 'just add latency routing' migration line-item becomes a new service.

aws.d: Distributions + origins + behaviors; pairs with S3 OAC.

azure.d: Modern SKU merges CDN + global L7 LB + WAF into one product.

gcp.d: Not standalone — a checkbox on a global LB backend.

usage: Cloud CDN isn't a product you 'create', it's enable_cdn = true on a backend service. Azure Front Door meanwhile ate three products (CDN, global LB, WAF) — map CloudFront + ALB + WAF to it together.

aws.d: Buckets, globally unique names, storage classes (Standard → Glacier).

azure.d: Two-level: Storage Account → Container → blobs. Tiers: Hot/Cool/Cold/Archive.

gcp.d: Buckets with Standard/Nearline/Coldline/Archive; dual-region buckets are unique to GCP.

usage: Azure's extra layer bites Terraform modules: the Storage Account (not the container) owns networking, keys, and replication settings — an S3-bucket-shaped module won't port cleanly.

aws.d: gp3/io2 volumes, zonal, attached to one instance (io2 supports multi-attach).

azure.d: Premium SSD v2 / Ultra tiers; disk is a first-class ARM resource.

gcp.d: Zonal or REGIONAL (synchronously replicated across two zones).

usage: GCP regional persistent disks replicate across zones automatically — an HA primitive AWS simply doesn't have in EBS; you'd build DRBD or use multi-attach hacks instead.

aws.d: EFS for NFS, FSx family for Windows/Lustre/NetApp.

azure.d: SMB-first (plus NFS); mounts with storage account keys or AD auth.

gcp.d: Managed NFS in tiers up to high-scale.

usage: Azure Files speaks SMB natively (Windows heritage); EFS and Filestore are NFS-first. A lift-and-shift of Windows file shares is genuinely easier on Azure.

aws.d: RDS runs stock engines; Aurora is the cloud-native MySQL/Postgres flavor.

azure.d: Azure SQL for MSSQL; Flexible Server for Postgres/MySQL.

gcp.d: Cloud SQL runs stock engines; AlloyDB is the Aurora-analog for Postgres.

usage: Map Aurora→AlloyDB, RDS→Cloud SQL/Flexible Server. On Azure, MSSQL is the home-turf engine with PaaS features (Managed Instance) that have no AWS twin.

aws.d: Single-digit-ms KV/doc store, on-demand or provisioned capacity.

azure.d: Multi-model, multi-region writes, five consistency levels, RU-based billing.

gcp.d: Firestore for doc workloads; Bigtable for wide-column at scale.

usage: Cosmos is broader than Dynamo (multiple APIs incl. a Mongo facade) and its Request Unit billing model is its own discipline — do the RU math before promising cost parity.

aws.d: Cluster-based (or Serverless); you think in nodes and WLM.

azure.d: Synapse dedicated pools; Microsoft is steering everything toward Fabric.

gcp.d: Fully serverless, storage/compute separated, billed per TB scanned or slots.

usage: BigQuery has no cluster to size — the operational model (and the cost failure mode: a careless SELECT * over petabytes) is completely different from Redshift capacity planning.

aws.d: Redis(-compatible)/Valkey and Memcached.

azure.n: Azure Cache for Redis / Managed Redis
azure.d: Tiered SKUs; Enterprise tiers run Redis Labs stack.

gcp.d: Redis and Memcached.

usage: Near 1:1 conceptually. Watch version/feature lag per tier — cluster mode, persistence, and auth options differ by SKU more than by cloud.

aws.d: You own node groups, VPC CNI quirks, and the add-on lifecycle. Control plane billed hourly.

azure.d: Free control plane (paid SLA tier); node pools; deep Entra integration.

gcp.d: The reference implementation. Autopilot mode removes node management entirely.

usage: Your EKS scars (CNI IP exhaustion, subnet tagging races, managed node group upgrades) mostly don't exist on GKE Autopilot — Google runs the nodes. AKS sits between: easier than EKS, less magic than Autopilot.

aws.d: Serverless data plane under ECS or EKS tasks.

azure.d: Built on KEDA + Dapr; scale-to-zero; ACI for raw single containers.

gcp.d: Request-driven containers, scale-to-zero, arguably the best DX of the three.

usage: Fargate is a data plane, not a product like the other two — the honest mapping is ECS+Fargate ↔ Container Apps ↔ Cloud Run. Cloud Run and Container Apps scale to zero; plain ECS services don't.

aws.d: Per-account registries, image scanning, pull-through cache.

azure.d: Registry with SKU tiers, geo-replication, and ACR Tasks (in-registry builds).

gcp.d: Successor to GCR; also hosts npm/Maven/apt — not just containers.

usage: Artifact Registry is a universal artifact store, not just Docker — one service can replace ECR + CodeArtifact. ACR Tasks can build images server-side, which has no direct ECR twin.

aws.d: 15-min max, event-source mappings, the richest trigger ecosystem.

azure.d: Hosting plans matter (Consumption vs Premium vs Dedicated); Durable Functions for workflows.

gcp.d: Formerly Cloud Functions; 2nd gen literally runs on Cloud Run infrastructure.

usage: Timeout/cold-start behavior is plan-dependent on Azure in a way AWS people don't expect — 'Lambda but 60 minutes' is only true on certain plans. GCP has quietly merged functions into Cloud Run.

aws.d: REST + HTTP APIs, usage plans, Lambda authorizers.

azure.d: Heavier: full API lifecycle product with developer portal and policies-as-XML.

gcp.d: API Gateway is the lightweight one; Apigee is the enterprise APIM analog.

usage: AWS API Gateway ↔ GCP API Gateway (light) and Azure APIM ↔ Apigee (heavy). Mapping AWS API Gateway to APIM 1:1 overprovisions — APIM's cheapest useful tiers cost real money at idle.

aws.d: State machines in ASL JSON; Standard vs Express.

azure.d: Logic Apps for low-code connectors; Durable Functions for code-first.

gcp.d: YAML-defined steps calling HTTP/connectors.

usage: Azure splits this in two: Logic Apps ≈ Step Functions + a huge SaaS connector library, Durable Functions ≈ code-first orchestration inside your function app. Pick per use case, not by name.

aws.d: Standard (at-least-once) and FIFO queues; dead-letter queues.

azure.d: Enterprise features: sessions, transactions, dedupe. (Storage Queues = the cheap basic tier.)

gcp.d: There is no separate queue product — a pull subscription plays the queue role.

usage: GCP simply doesn't have a queue/topic split: Pub/Sub is both SQS and SNS. Coming from AWS, resist looking for 'GCP SQS' — model it as topic + pull subscription with ordering keys if needed.

aws.d: Topics fanning out to SQS, Lambda, HTTP, mobile push.

azure.d: SB Topics for messages; Event Grid for discrete events with rich filtering.

gcp.d: Same product as the queue row; push subscriptions do the fan-out.

usage: Azure splits by semantics: Event Grid for 'something happened' (events), Service Bus for 'process this' (messages/commands). Mapping SNS blindly onto one of them loses that distinction.

aws.d: Kinesis is proprietary; MSK is managed Kafka.

azure.d: Kafka-protocol compatible endpoint out of the box.

gcp.d: Pub/Sub covers most streaming; Google now offers managed Kafka too.

usage: Event Hubs speaking the Kafka protocol natively is the killer translation fact — Kafka clients often just work with a connection-string change, no MSK-style cluster to run.

aws.d: JSON policies attached to identities; roles are assumed via STS.

azure.d: Identity lives in Entra (tenant-wide); RBAC = role definitions assigned at a scope (MG/sub/RG/resource).

gcp.d: Members get roles on resources; permissions inherit down Org → Folder → Project.

usage: The deepest break in the whole dictionary. AWS attaches policy documents to identities; Azure/GCP grant (identity, role, scope) triples with inheritance. 'Write me the IAM policy JSON' has no Azure translation — you pick a role definition and a scope instead.

aws.d: EC2/Lambda assume roles; IRSA wires roles to K8s service accounts.

azure.d: System- or user-assigned; the platform handles credentials invisibly.

gcp.d: A first-class identity that is ALSO an email address; attached to VMs, functions, GKE pods (Workload Identity).

usage: GCP service accounts are both an identity and a resource you can grant others permission to impersonate — richer but more foot-gunny than instance profiles. Azure Managed Identity is the most 'invisible' of the three.

aws.d: Rotation lambdas; Parameter Store as the budget option.

azure.d: One vault holds secrets AND keys AND certificates.

gcp.d: Versioned secrets, IAM-gated, simple.

usage: Key Vault bundles what AWS splits into Secrets Manager + KMS + ACM. One Azure resource, three AWS bills — decompose it when translating architectures, not names.

aws.d: CMKs, key policies (their own mini-IAM), envelope encryption everywhere.

azure.d: Keys live in vaults; HSM tiers for compliance.

gcp.d: Key rings → keys → versions; CMEK is the cross-service pattern.

usage: AWS KMS key policies are a parallel permission system that can lock out even account admins — Azure and GCP key access rides the normal RBAC/IAM plane instead. Fewer surprises, less isolation.

aws.d: Every API call, deliverable to S3/CloudWatch; org trails for all accounts.

azure.d: Control-plane operations per subscription; route via diagnostic settings.

gcp.d: Admin Activity always on and free; Data Access logs opt-in.

usage: GCP's Admin Activity logs are on by default and unpaid — the CloudTrail-setup checklist item doesn't exist. But Data Access logs (the expensive, chatty ones) are off until you enable them.

aws.d: Three products: findings hub, threat detection, config compliance.

azure.d: One umbrella product with per-resource-type Defender plans.

gcp.d: Standard vs Premium tiers; Premium adds threat detection.

usage: Azure and GCP bundle what AWS sells as three-plus SKUs. When someone asks for 'GuardDuty on Azure', the answer is a Defender plan toggle, not a new deployment.

aws.d: Namespaces, dimensions, composite alarms.

azure.d: Metric alerts + action groups (the SNS-topic analog for notifications).

gcp.d: Ex-Stackdriver; alerting policies + notification channels; MQL/PromQL.

usage: Azure 'action groups' ≈ the SNS topic your CloudWatch alarm notifies — a separate resource people forget to translate. GCP speaks PromQL natively, handy if you're Grafana-brained.

aws.d: Log groups/streams; Logs Insights for queries.

azure.d: THE central sink; queried with KQL (a genuinely different query language to learn).

gcp.d: Log buckets + Log Analytics (BigQuery-backed); logs route via sinks.

usage: On Azure, everything funnels into a Log Analytics Workspace and you must learn KQL — budget real ramp-up time for it; it's the biggest 'new language' cost of an AWS→Azure move.

aws.d: CUR (now Data Exports) to S3 + Athena is the serious path — the one your tfcost tooling world lives in.

azure.d: Cost Management (ex-Cloudyn) with FOCUS-format exports to storage.

gcp.d: Detailed usage lands in BigQuery tables; query with SQL directly.

usage: GCP's billing-in-BigQuery is the cleanest programmatic story — no Athena/CUR plumbing, just SQL. All three now support the FOCUS open billing schema, which is the real 'universal terminology' win in cost data.
